g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 4. Today we explore Blockchain Forks, pivotal events where protocols split, driving evolution and governance.
A fork is like a fork in the road—a blockchain splits into two paths. It's how decentralized networks upgrade and resolve disagreements.
g.text: A fork represents a pivotal event where a single blockchain protocol splits
into two separate, potentially divergent paths—much like a fork in the road.
Forks enable protocol upgrades, security fixes, and community decisions.

g.data-narration: Forks come in two main types: accidental (temporary) and intentional (permanent). Accidental forks resolve quickly using the longest chain rule.
Intentional forks are deliberate upgrades. They split into two categories: soft forks (backward-compatible) and hard forks (breaking changes).
A soft fork tightens rules, making them a subset of old rules. Old nodes can still participate since new blocks follow their rules too.
g.text: Mechanism: New rules are a SUBSET of old rules (tightens rules)
Example: P2SH (Pay-to-Script-Hash) in Bitcoin - old nodes see valid, new type of transaction

g.data-narration: A hard fork loosens or changes rules completely. Old nodes reject new blocks, permanently splitting the blockchain into two chains.
g.text: Mechanism: New rules are NOT a subset (loosens/changes rules)
Example: ETH/ETC Split - DAO Hack led to Ethereum vs Ethereum Classic chain

g.data-narration: Watch the visual difference: soft forks keep one chain, hard forks permanently split into two separate blockchains with different consensus.
For soft forks, only miners need to upgrade for full functionality—old nodes can still validate. For hard forks, ALL nodes must upgrade to stay in consensus.
g.text: Only miners need to upgrade for full functionality
All nodes must upgrade to stay on new chain

g.data-narration: Notable forks: Bitcoin's P2SH was a soft fork. Ethereum's DAO hack caused the famous ETH/ETC hard fork split. The Merge was Ethereum's PoW to PoS upgrade.
g.text: Hard Fork - PoW to PoS transition

g.data-narration: Why do communities fork? To add new features, fix security vulnerabilities, resolve philosophical disagreements, or even reverse controversial transactions.
Here's the complete comparison: soft forks tighten rules and stay compatible, hard forks loosen rules and create permanent splits.
g.text: Summary: Soft Fork vs Hard Fork

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Forks are essential for blockchain evolution. They reveal decentralized governance. Soft forks allow gradual upgrades; hard forks enable radical changes but carry split risk.
g.text: ✓ Forks are essential for blockchain evolution and protocol upgrades
✓ They highlight the decentralized, democratic nature of blockchain governance
✓ Soft forks = safer gradual upgrades | Hard forks = radical changes with split risk
